items.title: Feds hope new code list will cool payer No Surprises Act rage - BenefitsPRO
items.reason: This item directly addresses a federal regulatory development (a new code list) related to No Surprises Act implementation and payer compliance, which falls within the scope of CMS/HHS rulemaking affecting the Act's operation.
items.summary: Federal regulators have released a new code list intended to address ongoing compliance challenges and disputes between payers and providers under the No Surprises Act. The initiative targets frustration from payers struggling with implementation of the law's balance-billing and Independent Dispute Resolution requirements. Revenue-cycle teams should monitor this code list development, as it may clarify coding standards and reduce IDR disputes, potentially affecting claim processing workflows and dispute resolution timelines.

also.title: Guidance issued on new claims processing codes following independent dispute resolution updates - American Hospital Association

items.title: CEA Report, OhioHealth Settlement Continue White House and DOJ Focus on Payor-Provider Contracts - Morgan Lewis
items.reason: The item discusses DOJ enforcement activity and a settlement involving payer-provider contracts, which falls directly within payer litigation and regulatory enforcement scope.
items.summary: The White House and Department of Justice are maintaining focus on payer-provider contracts, as evidenced by a recent CEA report and an OhioHealth settlement. While the excerpt does not provide specific settlement terms, effective dates, or financial details, it indicates ongoing federal enforcement scrutiny of contracting practices between health insurers and healthcare providers. This development is relevant to revenue-cycle teams as it signals continued regulatory pressure on payer contracting practices and potential constraints on negotiation strategies between providers and insurance carriers.

items.title: FTC Reaches Second Insulin Pricing Settlement: Comparing the Caremark and ESI Orders - Mintz
items.reason: FTC enforcement actions against pharmacy benefit managers (PBMs) like Caremark and ESI directly impact payer operations and claims processing, which affects revenue-cycle teams' interactions with insurers and reimbursement practices.
items.summary: The FTC reached a second settlement regarding insulin pricing, this time with ESI (Express Scripts Inc.), following an earlier enforcement action against Caremark. Both orders target pharmacy benefit manager practices that allegedly contributed to rising insulin costs through opaque pricing mechanisms and formulary management. These FTC settlements establish regulatory constraints on how PBMs—which operate as payer affiliates or contractors—structure drug pricing and claims adjudication, directly affecting the reimbursement environment and claims denial patterns that revenue-cycle teams navigate. The enforcement actions signal increased regulatory scrutiny of PBM pricing practices that can influence which drugs are covered and at what reimbursement rates providers receive.
